items.myth: Binary search is faster than a hash table.
items.reality: Hash tables are O(1) average; binary search is O(log n). Pick a hash table for membership/lookup on unordered keys; pick binary search when the data is ordered or you need range queries.
items.example:
```python
set_lookup  # O(1) average
bisect      # O(log n) but enables ranges (`a..b`)
```

items.reality: An iterative loop is preferred — same complexity, no recursion-depth limit, often slightly faster.

items.myth: Binary search only works on numbers.
items.reality: It works on any total order — strings, tuples, custom objects with `<` defined — and on any monotonic predicate over an answer space.

items.myth: Once you've found the element, you've also handled duplicates.
items.reality: `bisect_left` returns the *first* possible insertion point; with duplicates you need both `bisect_left` and `bisect_right` to bound the run.
items.example:
```python
import bisect
xs = [1, 2, 2, 2, 3]
l = bisect.bisect_left(xs, 2)    # 1
r = bisect.bisect_right(xs, 2)   # 4
xs[l:r]                          # [2, 2, 2]
```

items.myth: Sorting in order to binary-search is always worthwhile.
items.reality: Sorting is O(n log n). For a single lookup on data you won't search again, a linear scan (O(n)) is cheaper. Sort once when many lookups will follow.
items.example:
```python
# One-off scan: just iterate.
# Many queries on the same data: sort once, then binary-search each.
```
